title: Why Ozone Holes Form Over the Poles

lesson.learn:
Ozone holes form over the poles because cold, isolated stratospheric air creates the right chemistry for ozone destruction. In winter, polar stratospheric clouds can form, and those clouds help convert chlorine and bromine compounds into reactive forms that break down ozone very quickly when sunlight returns. The result is a seasonal thinning of the ozone layer, especially over Antarctica.

The hole is not a literal gap in the atmosphere. It is a region where ozone concentrations become much lower than normal, allowing more ultraviolet radiation to reach the surface below.
lesson.simpler: The ozone hole happens when cold polar air sets up the chemistry that destroys ozone faster than it can be replaced.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Polar ozone holes form because cold stratospheric conditions activate chemicals that destroy ozone.
lesson.deeperDive:
The story begins with long-lived industrial chemicals such as CFCs. These compounds can drift high into the atmosphere, where ultraviolet light breaks them apart and releases chlorine. In the cold polar stratosphere, that chlorine becomes especially destructive once sunlight returns after winter darkness. The Antarctic vortex keeps the air isolated, so the chemistry is able to intensify.

International policy made a major difference here. The Montreal Protocol phased out many ozone-depleting substances, and the ozone layer has shown signs of recovery. That makes the ozone hole a rare example of a global environmental problem that science, diplomacy, and regulation have successfully addressed together.
lesson.funFact: The ozone hole is seasonal, so it grows and shrinks each year.
references: NASA Ozone Watch - ozone hole science
UNEP - Montreal Protocol and ozone recovery

people: Susan Solomon - major ozone depletion researcher
Mario Molina - helped explain CFC ozone loss
F. Sherwood Rowland - co-discovered the ozone threat from CFCs
Atmospheric chemists - scientists who track ozone chemistry
places: Antarctica - main region of the ozone hole
The Antarctic stratosphere - where polar clouds form
NASA and NOAA satellite centers - ozone monitoring hubs
The Arctic - a secondary site of ozone depletion
concepts: Stratosphere - upper atmospheric layer containing ozone
Polar vortex - isolated circulation around the poles
CFCs - chemicals that release ozone-destroying chlorine
Photochemical reaction - chemistry driven by sunlight
Ultraviolet radiation - high-energy sunlight blocked by ozone
